evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go` composes one `Runtime` from six embedded operation groups, while `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` centralizes default wiring for config, click, network, startram, file I/O, and image logic in one seam bundle path.

`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` mixes patch data model definitions, parsing adapters, apply/merge execution, registry lifecycle (`init`, validation, key map build), and type conversion helpers in one file-level unit.

`goseg/broadcast/state.go` combines in-memory state storage, scheduling bus publishing, collector routing, delivery orchestration, deep-copy policy, and JSON envelope serialization in one runtime/file boundary.

`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go` mixes low-level process execution seams, wifi-client querying, interface toggling, connection/disconnection flow, and captive sysctl mutation in one runtime type.

Nil/invalid receiver behavior is inconsistent in the same runtime-style APIs: `broadcastStateRuntime.GetState()` returns zero-value state on nil, `UpdateBroadcast()` returns `ErrBroadcastRuntimeRequired`, and `AddSystemTransitionError()` silently returns nil for empty message.
`logstreamSessionStore` similarly mixes no-op nil receiver methods with error-returning methods (`PublishSystemLog`), making caller expectations inconsistent across adjacent APIs.

`docker/events` exposes two broker constructors with different lifecycle semantics (`NewDefaultEventBroker()` follows mutable process default runtime; `NewTransitionBroker()` binds to its own runtime channels), but both are presented as default in-memory broker entry points.

In orchestration runtime dependency handling, missing callbacks produce different error families: `Runtime.UpdateConfig()` returns `errConfUpdateMissing`, `defaultRuntimeStartramOps()` returns local sentinel errors, while `StartupRuntime` methods return `seams.MissingRuntimeDependency(...)`.

Wi-Fi runtime methods use mixed failure contracts for parse/IO paths: `wifiDevices()` and `listSSIDs()` may return partial data with `ErrWiFiPartialResult`, while other methods treat errors as hard failures; this increases burden on callers to interpret severity.

`logstreamSessionStore` protects `store == nil` but not `store.systemLogBus == nil` before dereference in `SystemLogMessages()`/`PublishSystemLog()`, creating inconsistent behavior between explicit error paths and potential panic paths.

Config serialization contract has naming drift inside one model: `RuntimeConfig.CfgDir` uses JSON tag `CFG_DIR` while neighboring fields and update-patch fields use camelCase conventions (`apiVersion`, `endpointUrl`, `CfgDir` patch field).

Wi-Fi contract exposes dual-mode return semantics in core methods (`([]string, error)` where non-empty success payload can accompany an error), and tests explicitly assert this behavior; callers must know undocumented partial-result rules to use APIs safely.

Event broker contract naming suggests equivalent broker constructors, but one constructor is default-runtime indirection while the other is concrete runtime binding, creating behavior-level divergence not expressed by type signatures.

findings.summary: Orchestration runtime dependency wiring is over-aggregated, forcing unrelated concerns through one broad runtime shape.
evidence: `Runtime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, `RuntimeHealthOps`, `RuntimeStartupOps`, and `RuntimeStartramOps`, then merges all of them through shared options and dependency constructors.
`buildRuntimeSeamBundle` and `defaultRuntime*` builders wire multiple subsystem dependencies (network/config/click/startram/file/image) in one central construction path.
Tests in startup orchestration construct wide runtime override helpers to target behavior, indicating high setup breadth caused by the broad runtime dependency surface.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime dependency containers by bounded workflow (for example: startup runtime, container lifecycle runtime, startram runtime, health runtime) and provide narrower constructors/options so each call path receives only the operations it needs.

findings.summary: Config patch schema handling concentrates schema declaration, parsing, application, and merge policy into one monolithic unit.
evidence: `config_update_schema.go` defines patch structs, generic field factories, patch registry build/validation, parse helpers, and `buildConfigPatch` dispatch in a single file.
Field-level semantics span multiple domains (connectivity/runtime/startram/penpai/auth session) but are managed in one registry lifecycle path initialized at package load time.
`config_test.go` carries large multi-domain merge/update tests, which reflects that cross-domain patch behavior is tightly coupled to one schema engine.
findings.suggestion: Refactor patch handling into per-section modules (connectivity/runtime/startram/penpai/auth) that register field specs into a small shared registry interface; keep parse, apply, and merge responsibilities separated so adding one key does not require touching the global monolith.

findings.summary: Broadcast state runtime mixes state storage concerns with orchestration and transport-facing behavior.
evidence: `broadcastStateRuntime` owns locks and mutable state, scheduled pack bookkeeping, event bus publishing, collector dependency selection, and delivery runtime invocation.

The same file includes state mutation (`UpdateBroadcast`, `AddSystemTransitionError`), event signaling (`PublishSchedulePack`), collector orchestration (`collect*` helpers), and payload serialization (`GetStateJson`).
State tests include deep-copy semantics and transition-path behavior, showing behavior layers that are not isolated by role boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Separate broadcast concerns into explicit components: state store (copy/mutation), schedule/event signaling, collector aggregation, and transport serialization/delivery adapter; keep a thin coordinator that composes these components.

findings.summary: WiFi runtime bundles multiple operational roles (device discovery, connection control, and system policy mutation) into one type.
evidence: `wifiRuntime` includes process execution seams, wifi client seams, and ticker seams, while methods span read/query (`wifiDevices`, `listSSIDs`), control (`connect`, `disconnect`, `toggleDevice`), and system-level captive policy updates (`applyCaptiveRules`).
`toggleDevice` performs device resolution, radio state checks, and link manipulation in one method-level flow.
Test coverage spans many behavior families in one test file and C2C flow tests, indicating one runtime abstraction currently carries multiple distinct responsibilities.
findings.suggestion: Split WiFi responsibilities into smaller collaborators (scanner/query service, radio/link controller, captive-policy applier) and compose them in the higher-level C2C/system workflows.

findings.summary: Runtime-style APIs mix nil no-op, nil zero-value, and nil error contracts within the same call surface.
evidence: `broadcastStateRuntime.GetState()` returns empty state on nil, but `UpdateBroadcast()` returns `ErrBroadcastRuntimeRequired`.
`broadcastStateRuntime.AddSystemTransitionError("")` returns nil (silent skip) while other update methods generally error on invalid runtime.
`logstreamSessionStore` methods mostly no-op on nil receiver, while `PublishSystemLog` returns an error, creating mixed caller expectations.
findings.suggestion: Define and document one runtime-nil policy per subsystem (strict error or benign no-op), then normalize all exported methods to that policy; add focused tests asserting the chosen contract across all runtime methods.

findings.summary: Orchestration runtime reports missing dependencies through multiple unrelated error taxonomies.
evidence: `StartupRuntime` methods return `seams.MissingRuntimeDependency(...)` for nil callbacks.
`Runtime.UpdateConfig()` returns local sentinel `errConfUpdateMissing` for the same category of fault (missing injected callback).
`defaultRuntimeStartramOps()` returns separate local sentinels (`errStartramServicesLoaderMissing`, `errStartramRegionsLoaderMissing`) for dependency-not-configured faults.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single missing-dependency error contract (shared typed error or shared sentinel family with wrapping) for orchestration runtime seams, and migrate all callback-absence paths to it so callers can branch reliably.

findings.summary: Wi-Fi APIs return partial payloads with errors, but the contract is implicit and easy to misuse.
evidence: `wifiDevices()` and `listSSIDs()` can return non-empty slices together with wrapped `ErrWiFiPartialResult`.
`primaryWifiDevice()` may return a selected device plus an error when partial parse occurs.
Tests assert this mixed-success behavior, confirming callers must special-case partial-result errors to avoid dropping valid data.
findings.suggestion: Make partial-result behavior explicit at API boundary: either introduce a dedicated result type (`data`, `warnings`, `fatal`) or split methods into strict vs best-effort variants, and update caller/test naming to reflect this contract.

findings.summary: Runtime config JSON schema uses a convention outlier key that breaks local contract consistency.
evidence: `RuntimeConfig.CfgDir` is tagged as `json:"CFG_DIR"` while adjacent fields in the same config model use lower camel-case JSON keys.
Patch/update schema exposes `CfgDir` in code-level API terms without matching explicit normalization of the persistence key naming convention.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit schema normalization for this field (accept legacy `CFG_DIR` plus canonical `cfgDir`), document the canonical key, and add compatibility tests that enforce one stable public contract.

findings.summary: Event broker constructors with similar role names expose materially different runtime-binding semantics.
evidence: `NewDefaultEventBroker()` routes each call through mutable process defaults (`DefaultEventRuntime()`), so behavior changes when defaults are reset.
`NewTransitionBroker(bufferSize)` returns an `EventRuntime` bound to its own channels, unaffected by later default-runtime swaps.
These are both public constructors for the same broker interface, but signatures/naming do not communicate lifecycle-binding difference.
findings.suggestion: Rename constructors to encode binding semantics (for example, `NewGlobalDefaultBroker` vs `NewIsolatedRuntimeBroker`) and add API docs/tests that explicitly cover behavior after `SetDefaultEventRuntime`/`ResetDefaultEventRuntime`.
